past_medical_history: No prior seizures or neurological disease

medications.indication: Added as third AED for refractory seizures (not effective)

family_history: No family history of epilepsy, autoimmune disease, or neurological conditions
clinical_history.social_history: High school math teacher. Previously excellent work performance, now making frequent errors and has been placed on administrative leave. Married 5 years. Wife reports progressive personality changes over the past 2 months: irritable, paranoid, forgetful. Unintentional 5 kg weight loss over 4 months. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol use (currently abstinent due to AEDs). No illicit drug use.

presenting_complaint.hpi: 31-year-old right-handed Caucasian male math teacher referred for evaluation of refractory new-onset seizures despite 4 months of antiepileptic drug therapy. History began approximately 6 months ago with brief (<3 second) involuntary jerking of the right arm with simultaneous right-sided facial grimacing, occurring 20-40 times daily. His community neurologist diagnosed simple partial seizures (focal aware seizures) from presumed temporal lobe epilepsy based on a routine EEG showing 'mild temporal slowing' and an MRI reported as 'possible hippocampal signal change.' He was started on levetiracetam, then carbamazepine was added, then lacosamide — with no improvement in seizure frequency. Over the past 2 months, his wife reports progressive memory impairment (forgets recent conversations, gets lost driving familiar routes to work), personality change (increasingly irritable and suspicious, accused her of hiding things), sleep disturbance with violent dream enactment episodes (RBD-like behavior), and 5 kg unintentional weight loss. The brief involuntary arm/face episodes now occur 30-50 times daily. He was referred to this center for further evaluation of drug-resistant epilepsy.
presenting_complaint.duration: 6 months of seizures, 2 months of cognitive decline
presenting_complaint.severity: Progressive: seizures 30-50 times daily, now with cognitive impairment and personality change
associated_symptoms: Personality change with irritability and paranoia
Unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over 4 months
Right arm and face involuntary jerking episodes lasting 2-3 seconds, 30-50 times daily
Difficulty with work tasks (math calculations he previously handled easily)

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but slow to respond. Oriented to person and place but not to date (states the wrong month). MMSE 22/30 (lost points: date -1, day of week -1, delayed recall 0/3, serial sevens -3, missing 2 points). Slow processing speed. Unable to perform multi-step calculations (a math teacher who cannot do serial 7s). Affect somewhat flat.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII intact. No facial asymmetry between episodes. Pupils equal and reactive. Visual fields full.
neurological_exam.motor: During examination, witnessed 4 brief (2-3 second) episodes of right arm extension with right facial contraction — faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS). Between episodes, subtle right arm choreiform movements noted. Strength 5/5 bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. Normal muscle bulk and tone.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Bilateral lower extremity reflexes mildly brisk (3+). Upper extremity reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose normal bilaterally. Heel-to-shin normal. No dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait. No ataxia. Tandem gait normal.
neurological_exam.special_signs: Witnessed faciobrachial dystonic seizures (4 during 30-minute examination). Subtle right arm chorea between episodes.

findings.type: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity with hippocampal swelling
findings.location: Bilateral mesial temporal lobes, left greater than right, involving hippocampi and amygdalae
findings.size: Left hippocampus mildly swollen; right hippocampus at upper limit of normal volume

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Bilateral mesial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity, left > right, extending to amygdalae

findings.mass_effect: Mild hippocampal swelling left > right (not ATROPHY)

additional_observations: This is hippocampal SWELLING, NOT atrophy — an important distinction from mesial temporal sclerosis
Mesial temporal sclerosis (hippocampal sclerosis) shows ATROPHY and gliotic signal, not swelling
Bilateral involvement with swelling is more consistent with an inflammatory or autoimmune process than primary epilepsy
No mass lesion, no hemorrhagic changes, no enhancement to suggest HSV encephalitis
mri.impression: Bilateral mesial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity with mild left hippocampal swelling, left greater than right. This pattern is NOT consistent with hippocampal sclerosis (which shows atrophy). The bilateral swelling pattern raises concern for autoimmune or inflammatory limbic encephalitis. No mass, no enhancement, no hemorrhagic changes.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Bilateral hippocampal swelling and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity without atrophy is the classic MRI pattern
No hemorrhagic changes, no enhancement, subacute course over months (HSV is typically acute)
Hippocampal SWELLING contradicts MTS, which shows ATROPHY

recommended_actions: Autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (serum and CSF)
Check serum sodium (SIADH associated with LGI1 encephalitis)

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz theta-alpha activity (mildly slow, should be 8.5-13 Hz in a 31-year-old)
background.continuity: Continuous with moderate diffuse background slowing
background.symmetry: Mildly asymmetric with more prominent left temporal slowing

findings.morphology: Moderate amplitude sharp waves with after-going slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Interictal epileptiform discharges from left temporal region
Temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity, bilaterally but left-predominant

findings.morphology: Very brief (2-3 second) low-amplitude fast activity followed by generalized attenuation, coinciding with right arm extension and right facial contraction. Ictal onset is bilateral temporal, poorly lateralized. Duration is extremely short.
findings.clinical_correlation: These very brief (2-3 second) seizures with tonic-dystonic rather than typical focal temporal morphology are atypical for standard temporal lobe epilepsy. The extremely short duration and bilateral onset should raise suspicion for autoimmune etiology.

artifacts.impact: Brief obscuration during 2-3 second episodes but ictal onset visible

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderate diffuse background slowing, bilateral temporal sharp waves (left predominant), TIRDA, and 6 captured very brief seizures (2-3 seconds) with bilateral temporal onset and tonic-dystonic semiology. The seizure morphology (extremely brief, tonic-dystonic, bilateral onset) is atypical for standard temporal lobe epilepsy and raises concern for autoimmune encephalitis.
eeg.limitations: Brief seizure duration limits detailed analysis of ictal evolution.
recommended_actions: Consider autoimmune etiology given atypical seizure morphology

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. Normal 12-lead ECG. Normal QTc interval.

BMP.clinical_significance: Significant hyponatremia. This is a KEY diagnostic clue: hyponatremia occurs in approximately 60% of LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis cases due to SIADH. Hyponatremia is rare in primary epilepsy.
Low-normal BUN consistent with dilutional state

AED_Levels.clinical_significance: Therapeutic level — seizures are not controlled despite adequate AED levels. NOTE: Carbamazepine may worsen hyponatremia via SIADH.

labs.interpretation: Significant hyponatremia (Na 126 mEq/L) is the most notable laboratory finding. In the context of new-onset refractory seizures with cognitive decline, hyponatremia is a critical diagnostic clue for LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis (which causes SIADH in ~60% of cases). Carbamazepine is also a known cause of SIADH, but the degree of hyponatremia and clinical context warrant investigation for autoimmune etiology. Carbamazepine level is therapeutic, confirming that seizures are not controlled despite adequate AED dosing. Otherwise unremarkable labs.
abnormal_values_summary: Sodium: 126 mEq/L (significant hyponatremia — KEY diagnostic clue for LGI1 encephalitis)
Carbamazepine level: 8.2 mcg/mL (therapeutic — seizures not controlled despite adequate level)

Autoimmune_Encephalitis_Panel.clinical_significance: LGI1 antibodies POSITIVE in serum — diagnostic for LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis. LGI1 encephalitis is characterized by FBDS, cognitive decline, and hyponatremia.
LGI1 antibodies POSITIVE in CSF, confirming intrathecal antibody production
CASPR2 antibodies can co-occur with LGI1; negative here
Mildly elevated GAD65; nonspecific finding that can be seen in autoimmune encephalitis. High titers (>20,000) would suggest GAD65-mediated encephalitis specifically.

output.interpretation: LGI1 antibodies are POSITIVE in both serum (1:64) and CSF (1:16), confirming the diagnosis of LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis. This explains the faciobrachial dystonic seizures, cognitive decline, personality change, hyponatremia, and AED-refractory seizures. NMDAR and CASPR2 antibodies are negative. Mildly elevated GAD65 is a nonspecific finding. Diagnosis: LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis.

special_studies.clinical_significance: No evidence of CNS lymphoma or carcinomatous meningitis

output.interpretation: CSF analysis reveals normal cell count (WBC 5), normal protein, and normal glucose. IMPORTANT: Normal CSF cell count does NOT exclude LGI1 encephalitis — unlike NMDAR encephalitis, LGI1 encephalitis frequently has a normal CSF profile. LGI1 antibodies are positive in CSF. HSV PCR negative. No malignant cells.
output.clinical_correlation: Normal CSF cellular profile with positive LGI1 antibodies is characteristic of LGI1 encephalitis. A normal cell count should not provide false reassurance against autoimmune encephalitis.

findings.location: Bilateral hippocampi and amygdalae, left > right

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly increased FDG uptake in bilateral mesial temporal structures (hippocampi and amygdalae), left > right, significantly above cortical average

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, occipital lobes, basal ganglia, thalami, cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Normal and symmetric FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Medial temporal HYPERMETABOLISM is characteristic of autoimmune encephalitis (active inflammation)
In contrast, temporal lobe EPILEPSY shows interictal HYPOMETABOLISM — the opposite pattern
This FDG-PET pattern strongly supports autoimmune limbic encephalitis
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bilateral mesial temporal HYPERMETABOLISM (increased FDG uptake in hippocampi and amygdalae), which is characteristic of autoimmune limbic encephalitis. This pattern is the OPPOSITE of primary temporal lobe epilepsy, which shows interictal hypometabolism. No tumor identified. Findings are concordant with LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Bilateral medial temporal hypermetabolism is characteristic of active autoimmune inflammation
Primary TLE would show interictal HYPOMETABOLISM, not hypermetabolism

recommended_actions: Initiate immunotherapy based on confirmed LGI1 encephalitis
Consider repeat PET after treatment to assess response

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Clear lungs, no masses or nodules. No thymoma. Normal mediastinum.
CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.clinical_significance: No thymoma (associated with some autoimmune encephalitides)

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Normal liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas. No masses.
Normal. No testicular mass or teratoma.

output.interpretation: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis is unremarkable. No thymoma, teratoma, or other tumor identified. LGI1 encephalitis is typically NOT paraneoplastic (unlike NMDAR encephalitis which is commonly associated with ovarian teratoma). The absence of a tumor is expected and consistent with the diagnosis of LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis.

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Severely impaired verbal learning — consistent with bilateral hippocampal dysfunction
Profound anterograde memory impairment — virtually no delayed recall
Impaired visual memory, consistent with bilateral hippocampal involvement
Impaired semantic fluency, consistent with temporal lobe dysfunction

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals severe anterograde memory impairment (CVLT-II delayed recall 2/16) with impaired verbal and visual memory, impaired verbal fluency, and reduced attention and executive function. The pattern is consistent with bilateral hippocampal dysfunction, which is characteristic of limbic encephalitis. The severity of memory impairment is disproportionate to the patient's young age and premorbid function (math teacher). This profile supports the diagnosis of autoimmune limbic encephalitis affecting bilateral mesial temporal structures.
abnormal_values_summary: CVLT-II Delayed Recall: 2/16 (severe anterograde memory impairment)
BVMT-R Total Learning: T-score 28 (impaired visual memory)
Verbal Fluency impaired (FAS T-score 34, Animals T-score 30)
Trail Making A and B both impaired

differential.key_distinguishing: FBDS are nearly pathognomonic for LGI1 encephalitis; hippocampal SWELLING (not sclerosis) on MRI; profound hyponatremia (Na 126); progressive cognitive decline; poor response to 3 AEDs; FDG-PET showing hypermetabolism (epilepsy shows hypometabolism).
NMDAR antibodies negative in both serum and CSF. FBDS are specific for LGI1, not NMDAR. No psychosis, dyskinesias, or autonomic instability typical of NMDAR encephalitis.

Subacute course over months (HSV is typically acute over days with fever). No hemorrhagic changes on MRI. CSF HSV PCR negative. Bilateral symmetric involvement rather than unilateral hemorrhagic necrosis.
No enhancement or mass on MRI. Bilateral symmetric temporal involvement. LGI1 antibodies positive confirming autoimmune etiology.

optimal_actions.action: Recognize faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS) — brief <3 second arm+face episodes occurring 20-50 times daily — as pathognomonic for LGI1 encephalitis
Check serum sodium — hyponatremia is a hallmark of LGI1 encephalitis due to SIADH
Order brain MRI to characterize temporal lobe pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal swelling (not atrophy) suggesting autoimmune process

optimal_actions.action: Order LGI1 and CASPR2 antibodies in BOTH serum and CSF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: LGI1 antibodies positive in serum (1:64) and CSF (1:16)

optimal_actions.action: Order CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, HSV PCR, and cytology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cell count (typical for LGI1), LGI1 positive in CSF, HSV PCR negative

optimal_actions.action: Start immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x 5 days, followed by IVIG or plasma exchange
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in FBDS frequency and cognitive function within days to weeks

optimal_actions.action: Consider AED taper once immunotherapy is initiated (seizures in LGI1 respond to immunotherapy, not AEDs)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gradual AED reduction as seizures respond to immunotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Monitor for progression to full limbic encephalitis; plan long-term immunosuppression if needed
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Long-term monitoring with repeat MRI and antibody titers

critical_actions: Recognize faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS) as pathognomonic for LGI1 encephalitis
Order LGI1 and CASPR2 antibodies (serum AND CSF)
Check sodium — hyponatremia is a hallmark of LGI1 encephalitis
Start immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone followed by IVIG or PLEX)
Consider AED taper once immunotherapy is initiated (seizures respond to immunotherapy, not AEDs)
Monitor for progression to full limbic encephalitis
contraindicated_actions: Adding more AEDs without considering autoimmune etiology — the seizures will not respond
Performing epilepsy surgery evaluation without checking autoimmune antibodies
Attributing refractory seizures to 'non-compliance' without investigating the underlying etiology
Correcting hyponatremia rapidly (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome — correct slowly, <8 mEq/L per 24 hours)
key_reasoning_points: Faciobrachial dystonic seizures (brief <3 second arm+face episodes, 20-50 per day) are nearly pathognomonic for LGI1 encephalitis — they precede full limbic encephalitis in most cases
Hyponatremia (Na 126) is present in approximately 60% of LGI1 cases due to SIADH — this is rare in primary epilepsy and is a critical diagnostic clue
Bilateral hippocampal SWELLING (not atrophy) distinguishes autoimmune encephalitis from epilepsy-related hippocampal sclerosis, which shows ATROPHY
FDG-PET showing medial temporal HYPERMETABOLISM is characteristic of autoimmune encephalitis (active inflammation); primary epilepsy shows interictal HYPOMETABOLISM — the opposite pattern
Poor response to 3 AEDs at therapeutic levels should trigger investigation for autoimmune etiology, not simply addition of more AEDs
Normal CSF cell count does NOT exclude LGI1 encephalitis — unlike NMDAR encephalitis, which usually has pleocytosis, LGI1 often has a bland CSF

Cognitive decline and personality change (memory impairment, irritability, paranoia) indicate limbic encephalitis beyond simple epilepsy — a seizure disorder alone does not explain progressive cognitive decline in a 31-year-old

red_herrings.intended_effect: Confirms the initial diagnosis of temporal lobe epilepsy and reinforces the AED-treatment approach
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: EEG abnormalities in LGI1 encephalitis can perfectly mimic temporal lobe epilepsy. The very brief seizure duration (2-3 seconds) and dystonic semiology (not typical temporal lobe automatisms) should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis.

red_herrings.data_point: Hippocampal signal abnormality on MRI reported as 'possible hippocampal signal change'
red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests mesial temporal sclerosis (hippocampal sclerosis) consistent with primary epilepsy
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: The MRI shows hippocampal SWELLING (enlarged volume, not atrophy). Hippocampal sclerosis shows ATROPHY and gliosis. Swelling with bilateral involvement points to an inflammatory or autoimmune process.

red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests adequate AED treatment is in place and that adding another AED might help
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Therapeutic AED levels with no seizure control means the underlying cause is autoimmune, not a primary epileptic process. The seizures will only respond to immunotherapy. Additionally, carbamazepine may be worsening the hyponatremia via its own SIADH effect.

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis initially misdiagnosed as temporal lobe epilepsy. Key clues: FBDS (pathognomonic), hyponatremia, hippocampal swelling (not sclerosis), cognitive decline, and AED refractoriness. Requires recognition that refractory new-onset seizures with cognitive decline warrant autoimmune workup.
